metrics.title: Academic Metrics
metrics.subtitle: Indicators for textual transmission analysis
metrics.stabilizationDescription: The New Testament reached 90% coverage by century {century}

history.title: Historical Context
history.subtitle: Transmission of the New Testament text
history.papyriOriginText: The New Testament papyri come mainly from Egypt, where the dry climate preserved organic materials for centuries. Notable discoveries include those from Oxyrhynchus (1897–1907), which revealed thousands of fragments; the Chester Beatty Papyri (1930s), with nearly complete codices of Paul and the Gospels; and the Bodmer Papyri (1950s), including P66 and P75 with Johannine and Gospel texts of great antiquity.
history.codexDevelopmentText: The transition from the scroll (volumen) to the codex (book with pages) was decisive for the transmission of the text. The codex allowed multiple books to be gathered in a single volume, facilitated consultation and comparison of passages, and became the standard format for Christian writings as early as the 2nd century. P52 (c. 125–175), the oldest NT fragment, is already in codex format.
history.greatCodexText: The 4th century produced the great uncial manuscripts containing the complete or nearly complete NT: the Codex Sinaiticus (א, 01), discovered at St. Catherine's Monastery; the Codex Vaticanus (B, 03), preserved in the Vatican Apostolic Library; and the Codex Alexandrinus (A, 02), of Alexandrian provenance. These witnesses are fundamental for textual criticism and the reconstruction of the original text.
history.textualExpansion: Textual Expansion up to the 10th Century
history.textualExpansionText: From the 5th century onward, minuscule (cursive) script gradually replaced uncial in manuscripts. Scriptoria in Byzantine monasteries, such as those in Constantinople and Mount Athos, copied and preserved the text on a large scale. The majority of the more than 5,800 Greek NT manuscripts are minuscules from the 9th–16th centuries, witnessing the expansion and stabilization of the Byzantine textual tradition.

manuscriptCount.title: How Many Manuscripts Exist?
manuscriptCount.subtitle: Total Greek New Testament manuscripts
manuscriptCount.gregoryAlandDescription: The Gregory-Aland cataloging system is the international standard for identifying Greek New Testament manuscripts. Created by Caspar René Gregory and updated by Kurt Aland, it classifies manuscripts into four categories:
manuscriptCount.papyriDescription: Written on papyrus, these are the oldest witnesses. Designated with the prefix P followed by a number (e.g.: P52, P66, P75). Most come from Egypt, dating from the 2nd–7th centuries.
manuscriptCount.uncialsDescription: Parchment manuscripts written in uppercase (uncial) letters. They include the great codices such as Sinaiticus (01), Vaticanus (03), and Alexandrinus (02). Dating from the 3rd–10th centuries.
manuscriptCount.minusculesDescription: Manuscripts in cursive minuscule script, predominant from the 9th century onward. They represent the majority of NT manuscripts, numbered from 1 to 2900+.
manuscriptCount.lectionariesDescription: Liturgical manuscripts containing NT passages organized for public reading in worship. Designated with the prefix l (e.g.: l1, l2).

sources.title: Data Sources
sources.subtitle: Origin, methodology, and data limitations
sources.primarySourceText1: The manuscriptological data is obtained from the {ntvmr}, maintained by the Institute for New Testament Textual Research (INTF) at the University of Münster, Germany.
sources.primarySourceText2: The NTVMR API exposes transcriptions in TEI/XML format. For each manuscript, the system queries the transcription endpoint with the parameter {formatParam}, which returns XML markup with {abElement} elements indicating which verses are present in the manuscript.
sources.primarySourceText3: The ingestion flow parses the XML, extracts the present verses, and links each one to the manuscript record in the database. When the NTVMR API is unavailable or does not return data for a specific manuscript, the system uses ranges defined in seed data as a fallback.
sources.gregoryAlandText1: The Gregory-Aland cataloging system assigns unique identifiers to each Greek New Testament manuscript. The numbering follows conventions by type:
sources.gregoryAlandUncials: Uncials: numbering 01–0323+ (or Greek/Latin letters for the oldest)
sources.gregoryAlandText2: The catalog is maintained by the INTF and updated as new manuscripts are discovered or reclassified.
sources.datingMethodologyText1: For manuscripts with a date range (e.g., "2nd/3rd century"), the system adopts a {conservative} approach: it uses the earliest century of the range ({centuryMin}). This means that if a manuscript is dated between the 2nd and 3rd centuries, it is counted as a 2nd-century attestation.
sources.datingMethodologyText2: The {cumulativeCoverage} for a given century N includes all manuscripts from centuries 1 to N. Thus, 5th-century coverage includes 2nd-century papyri, 3rd-century uncials, and so on. A verse appearing in multiple manuscripts is counted only once (deduplication).
sources.limitationFragmentary: Many papyri and uncials are fragments that preserve only portions of books. The system accurately records which verses are present, but smaller fragments contribute less to total coverage.
sources.limitationDating: The dating of ancient manuscripts is based on paleographic analysis (writing style), which has a margin of error of 50–100 years. The dates used are consensus scholarly estimates, not absolute dates.
sources.limitationVariants: This system measures {coverage} (whether a verse exists in at least one manuscript), not {textualVariation} (differences between manuscripts). The existence of a verse in a manuscript does not imply that its text is identical to that of other manuscripts.

faq.title: Academic FAQ
faq.subtitle: Frequently asked questions about NT manuscriptology
faq.q1: Was the New Testament altered over the centuries?
faq.a1: The manuscript tradition of the NT is extraordinarily rich. With more than 5,800 Greek manuscripts, it is possible to reconstruct the original text with a high degree of confidence. Most textual variants are trivial copying errors (spelling, word order) that do not affect meaning. Significant variants represent less than 1% of the text, and no central Christian doctrine depends on disputed passages.
faq.q2: How many verses do not appear in the oldest manuscripts?
faq.a2_part1: In manuscripts up to the 3rd century (papyri), NT coverage already exceeds 40% of verses. By the 5th century, with the great uncial codices, coverage surpasses 90%. Verses "absent" from the oldest manuscripts does not mean they did not exist — only that the manuscripts containing them did not survive. Check the
faq.a2_part2: to visualize coverage evolution by century.
faq.q3: Are there NT books that only appear in the 4th century?
faq.a3: There are no NT books that only appear from the 4th century onward. All 27 books have earlier manuscript attestation. However, shorter books like 2 John, 3 John, and Jude have fewer ancient witnesses simply because they are short (1 chapter) — there are fewer opportunities for preservation. The Gospels and Pauline epistles have solid papyrological attestation since the 2nd century.
faq.q4: What is the Pericope of the Adulteress?
faq.a4: The passage of John 7:53–8:11 (Pericope Adulterae) does not appear in the oldest manuscripts, such as P66, P75, Sinaiticus, and Vaticanus. It is considered by most scholars to be a later addition to the Gospel of John, although it may preserve an authentic tradition. This system records the exact coverage of verses, allowing you to verify in which manuscripts the passage appears.
faq.q5: What is the oldest New Testament manuscript?
faq.a5_part1: P52 (Rylands Papyrus 457) is generally considered the oldest NT manuscript, dated between 125–175 AD. It contains fragments of John 18:31–33 and 18:37–38. Other early papyri include P90 (John, 2nd c.), P104 (Matthew, 2nd c.), and the Chester Beatty Papyri set (P45, P46, P47) from the 3rd century. Explore the details on the
faq.apologistQuestions: Common questions from a Christian apologist
faq.q6: How many manuscripts do we have compared to other ancient works?
faq.a6: The NT has more than 5,800 Greek manuscripts, plus ~10,000 Latin manuscripts and thousands in other languages (Syriac, Coptic, etc.). For comparison: Homer's Iliad has ~1,800 manuscripts; Plato's works, ~250; Tacitus's Annals, ~20 copies. No work from Antiquity comes close to the NT in volume of manuscript evidence.
faq.q7: Is the NT the best-attested ancient text?
faq.a7: Yes, both in number of manuscripts and in temporal proximity to the original. The interval between the composition of the NT books (c. 50–100 AD) and the earliest manuscripts (c. 125–200 AD) is only 25–150 years. For most classical works, this interval is 800–1400 years.
faq.q8: What is the century of textual stabilization?
faq.a8_part1: The concept of "stabilization" indicates the century in which cumulative coverage reaches 90% of verses. For the NT as a whole, this generally occurs between the 4th and 5th centuries. Check the
faq.a8_part2: to see the stabilization century for each individual book.
faq.q9: What is the coverage percentage up to the 3rd century?
faq.a9_part1: Coverage varies by book. The Gospels of John and Matthew have significant coverage thanks to papyri like P66, P75, P45. Pauline epistles are also well attested via P46. Shorter books like 2 Peter and Jude have less attestation in this period. Use the
faq.a9_part2: with the century slider to explore the data in real time.
faq.a10_part1: The Fragmentation Index measures how fragmentary the attestation of a book is. Calculated as {formula}. Values close to 0 indicate few manuscripts per verse (high fragmentation); values close to 1 indicate many manuscripts attesting each verse (low fragmentation). Available on the
faq.a11: The growth rate of coverage between consecutive centuries. Calculated as {formula}. A high growth rate from the 3rd to 4th century reflects the contribution of the great uncial codices. Decreasing rates indicate that coverage is stabilizing.
faq.a12: The ratio of covered verses to number of manuscripts: {formula}. High values indicate that each manuscript contributes many unique verses. Low values indicate redundancy (many manuscripts covering the same verses).
faq.a13_part1: Cumulative coverage for century N includes all manuscripts from centuries 1 to N. A verse is "covered" if it appears in at least one manuscript up to the century considered. Each verse is counted only once, regardless of how many manuscripts contain it. This means that coverage is monotonically increasing — it never decreases from one century to the next.
